sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: ὑετοῦ ἀμφιβάλῃ ἀλέην · κεφαλῆφι δʼ ὕπερθεν
sections.translation: and to keep off the rain . On your head above wear a shaped cap of felt to keep your ears from getting wet , for the dawn is chill when Boreas has once made his onslaught , and at dawn a fruitful mist is spread over the earth from starry heaven upon the fields of blessed men :

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: ὅστε ἀρυσάμενος ποταμῶν ἄπο αἰεναόντων ,
sections.translation: it is drawn from the ever-flowing rivers and is raised high above the earth by windstorm , and sometimes it turns to rain towards evening , and sometimes to wind when Thracian Boreas huddles the thick clouds . Finish your work and return home ahead of him ,

sections.label: Section 555
sections.original: μή ποτέ σʼ οὐρανόθεν σκοτόεν νέφος ἀμφικαλύψῃ ,
sections.translation: and do not let the dark cloud from heaven wrap round you and make your body clammy and soak your clothes . Avoid it ; for this is the hardest month , wintry , hard for sheep and hard for men . In this season let your oxen have half their usual food , but let your man have more ;

sections.label: Section 560
sections.original: ἁρμαλιῆς · μακραὶ γὰρ ἐπίρροθοι εὐφρόναι εἰσίν .
sections.translation: for the helpful nights are long . Observe all this until the year is ended and you have nights and days of equal length , and Earth , the mother of all , bears again her various fruit . When Zeus has finished

sections.label: Section 565
sections.original: χειμέριʼ ἐκτελέσῃ Ζεὺς ἤματα , δή ῥα τότʼ ἀστὴρ
sections.translation: sixty wintry days after the solstice , then the star Arcturus [*] leaves the holy stream of Ocean and first rises brilliant at dusk . After him the shrilly wailing daughter of Pandion , the swallow , appears to men when spring is just beginning .

sections.label: Section 570
sections.original: τὴν φθάμενος οἴνας περταμνέμεν · ὣς γὰρ ἄμεινον .
sections.translation: Before she comes , prune the vines , for it is best so . 571 But when the House-carrier [*] climbs up the plants from the earth to escape the Pleiades , then it is no longer the season for digging vineyards , but to whet your sickles and rouse up your slaves . Avoid shady seats and sleeping until dawn

sections.label: Section 575
sections.original: ὥρῃ ἐν ἀμήτου , ὅτε τʼ ἠέλιος χρόα κάρφει .
sections.translation: in the harvest season , when the sun scorches the body . Then be busy and bring home your fruits , getting up early to make your livelihood sure . For dawn takes away a third part of your work , dawn advances a man on his journey and advances him in his work ,—

sections.label: Section 580
sections.original: ἠώς , ἥτε φανεῖσα πολέας ἐπέβησε κελεύθου
sections.translation: dawn which appears and sets many men on their road , and puts yokes on many oxen . But when the artichoke flowers , [*] and the chirping grass-hopper sits in a tree and pours down his shrill song continually from under his wings in the season of wearisome heat ,

sections.label: Section 585
sections.original: τῆμος πιόταταί τʼ αἶγες καὶ οἶνος ἄριστος ,
sections.translation: then goats are plumpest and wine sweetest ; women are most wanton , but men are feeblest , because Sirius parches head and knees and the skin is dry through heat . But at that time let me have a shady rock and wine of Biblis ,

sections.label: Section 590
sections.original: μάζα τʼ ἀμολγαίη γάλα τʼ αἰγῶν σβεννυμενάων ,
sections.translation: a clot of curds and milk of drained goats with the flesh of a heifer fed in the woods , that has never calved , and of firstling kids ; then also let me drink bright wine , sitting in the shade , when my heart is satisfied with food , and so , turning my head to face the fresh Zephyr ,

sections.label: Section 595
sections.original: κρήνης τʼ αἰενάου καὶ ἀπορρύτου , ἥτʼ ἀθόλωτος ,
sections.translation: from the everflowing spring which pours down unfouled , thrice pour an offering of water , but make a fourth libation of wine . Set your slaves to winnow Demeter ' s holy grain , when strong Orion [*] first appears , on a smooth threshing-floor in an airy place .

sections.label: Section 600
sections.original: μέτρῳ δʼ εὖ κομίσασθαι ἐν ἄγγεσιν · αὐτὰρ ἐπὴν δὴ
sections.translation: Then measure it and store it in jars . And so soon as you have safely stored all your stuff indoors , I bid you put your bondman out of doors and seek out a servant-girl with no children ;— for a servant with a child to nurse is troublesome . And look after the dog with jagged teeth ; do not grudge him his food ,

sections.label: Section 605
sections.original: μή ποτέ σʼ ἡμερόκοιτος ἀνὴρ ἀπὸ χρήμαθʼ ἕληται .
sections.translation: or some time the Day-sleeper [*] may take your stuff . Bring in fodder and litter so as to have enough for your oxen and mules . After that , let your men rest their poor knees and unyoke your pair of oxen . 609 But when Orion and Sirius are come into midheaven ,

sections.label: Section 610
sections.original: οὐρανόν , Ἀρκτοῦρον δʼ ἐσίδῃ ῥοδοδάκτυλος Ηώς ,
sections.translation: and rosy-fingered Dawn sees Arcturus , [*] then cut off all the grape-clusters , Perses , and bring them home . Show them to the sun ten days and ten nights : then cover them over for five , and on the sixth day draw off into vessels the gifts of joyful Dionysus . But when

sections.label: Section 615
sections.original: Πληιάδες θʼ Ὑάδες τε τό τε σθένος Ὠαρίωνος
sections.translation: the Pleiades and Hyades and strong Orion begin to set , [*] then remember to plough in season : and so the completed year [*] will fitly pass beneath the earth . But if desire for uncomfortable sea-faring seize you when the Pleiades plunge into the misty sea [*]

sections.label: Section 620
sections.original: φεύγουσαι πίπτωσιν ἐς ἠεροειδέα πόντον ,
sections.translation: to escape Orion ' s rude strength , then truly gales of all kinds rage . Then keep ships no longer on the sparkling sea , but be sure to till the land as I bid you . Haul up your ship upon the land and pack it closely with stones

sections.label: Section 625
sections.original: πάντοθεν , ὄφρʼ ἴσχωσʼ ἀνέμων μένος ὑγρὸν ἀέντων ,
sections.translation: all round to keep off the power of the winds which blow damply , and draw out the bilge-plug so that the rain of heaven may not rot it . Put away all the tackle and fittings in your house , and stow the wings of the sea-going ship neatly , and hang up the well-shaped rudder over the smoke .

sections.label: Section 630
sections.original: αὐτὸς δʼ ὡραῖον μίμνειν πλόον , εἰσόκεν ἔλθῃ ·
sections.translation: You yourself wait until the season for sailing is come , and then haul your swift ship down to the sea and stow a convenient cargo in it , so that you may bring home profit , even as your father and mine , foolish Perses , used to sail on shipboard because he lacked sufficient livelihood . And one day he came to this very place , crossing over a great stretch of sea ;

sections.label: Section 635
sections.original: ὅς ποτε καὶ τῇδʼ ἦλθε , πολὺν διὰ πόντον ἀνύσσας ,
sections.translation: he left Aeolian Cyme and fled , not from riches and substance , but from wretched poverty which Zeus lays upon men , and he settled near Helicon in a miserable hamlet ,

sections.label: Section 640
sections.original: Ἄσκρῃ , χεῖμα κακῇ , θέρει ἀργαλέῃ , οὐδέ ποτʼ ἐσθλῇ .
sections.translation: Ascra , which is bad in winter , sultry in summer , and good at no time . 641 But you , Perses , remember all works in their season but sailing especially . Admire a small ship , but put your freight in a large one ; for the greater the lading , the greater will be your piled gain ,

sections.label: Section 645
sections.original: ἔσσεται , εἴ κʼ ἄνεμοί γε κακὰς ἀπέχωσιν ἀήτας .
sections.translation: if only the winds will keep back their harmful gales . If ever you turn your misguided heart to trading and wish to escape from debt and joyless hunger , I will show you the measures of the loud-roaring sea , though I have no skill in sea-faring nor in ships ;

sections.label: Section 650
sections.original: οὐ γάρ πώ ποτε νηί γʼ ἐπέπλων εὐρέα πόντον ,
sections.translation: for never yet have I sailed by ship over the wide sea , but only to Euboea from Aulis where the Achaeans once stayed through much storm when they had gathered a great host from divine Hellas for Troy , the land of fair women .

sections.label: Section 655
sections.original: Χαλκίδα τʼ εἲς ἐπέρησα · τὰ δὲ προπεφραδμένα πολλὰ
sections.translation: Then I crossed over to Chalcis , to the games of wise Amphidamas where the sons of the great-hearted hero proclaimed and appointed prizes . And there I boast that I gained the victory with a song and carried off a handled tripod which I dedicated to the Muses of Helicon , in the place where they first set me in the way of clear song .

sections.label: Section 660
sections.original: τόσσον τοι νηῶν γε πεπείρημαι πολυγόμφων ·
sections.translation: Such is all my experience of many-pegged ships ; nevertheless I will tell you the will of Zeus who holds the aegis ; for the Muses have taught me to sing in marvellous song . Fifty days after the solstice , [*] when the season of wearisome heat is come to an end ,

sections.label: Section 665
sections.original: ὡραῖος πέλεται θνητοῖς πλόος · οὔτε κε νῆα
sections.translation: is the right time for men to go sailing . Then you will not wreck your ship , nor will the sea destroy the sailors , unless Poseidon the Earth-Shaker be set upon it , or Zeus , the king of the deathless gods , wish to slay them ; for the issues of good and evil alike are with them .

sections.label: Section 670
sections.original: τῆμος δʼ εὐκρινέες τʼ αὖραι καὶ πόντος ἀπήμων ·
sections.translation: At that time the winds are steady , and the sea is harmless . Then trust in the winds without care , and haul your swift ship down to the sea and put all the freight on board ; but make all haste you can to return home again and do not wait till the time of the new wine and autumn rain and

sections.label: Section 675
sections.original: καὶ χειμῶνʼ ἐπιόντα Νότοιό τε δεινὰς ἀήτας ,
sections.translation: oncoming storms with the fierce gales of Notus who accompanies the heavy autumn rain of Zeus and stirs up the sea and makes the deep dangerous . 678 Another time for men to go sailing is in spring when a man first sees leaves on the topmost shoot of a fig-tree as large as the foot-print that a crow makes ;

sections.label: Section 680
sections.original: ἴχνος ἐποίησεν , τόσσον πέταλʼ ἀνδρὶ φανείῃ
sections.translation: then the sea is passable , and this is the spring sailing time . For my part I do not praise it , for my heart does not like it . Such a sailing is snatched , and you will hardly avoid mischief . Yet in their ignorance

sections.label: Section 685
sections.original: ἄνθρωποι ῥέζουσιν ἀιδρείῃσι νόοιο ·
sections.translation: men do even this , for wealth means life to poor mortals ; but it is fearful to die among the waves . But I bid you consider all these things in your heart as I say . Do not put all your goods in hollow ships ;

sections.label: Section 690
sections.original: ἀλλὰ πλέω λείπειν , τὰ δὲ μείονα φορτίζεσθαι .
sections.translation: leave the greater part behind , and put the lesser part on board ; for it is a bad business to meet with disaster among the waves of the sea , as it is bad if you put too great a load on your wagon and break the axle , and your goods are spoiled . Observe due measure : and proportion is best in all things .

sections.label: Section 695
sections.original: ὡραῖος δὲ γυναῖκα τεὸν ποτὶ οἶκον ἄγεσθαι ,
sections.translation: Bring home a wife to your house when you are of the right age , while you are not far short of thirty years nor much above ; this is the right age for marriage . Let your wife have been grown up four years , and marry her in the fifth . Marry a maiden , so that you can teach her careful ways ,

sections.label: Section 700
sections.original: τὴν δὲ μάλιστα γαμεῖν , ἥ τις σέθεν ἐγγύθι ναίει ,
sections.translation: and especially marry one who lives near you , but look well about you and see that your marriage will not be a joke to your neighbors . For a man wins nothing better than a good wife , and , again , nothing worse than a bad one , a greedy soul who

sections.label: Section 705
sections.original: εὕει ἄτερ δαλοῖο καὶ ὠμῷ γήραϊ δῶκεν .
sections.translation: roasts her man without fire , strong though he may be , and brings him to a raw [*] old age . 706 Be careful to avoid the anger of the deathless gods . Do not make a friend equal to a brother ; but if you do , do not wrong him first , and do not lie to please the tongue . But if he wrongs you first ,

sections.label: Section 710
sections.original: ἤ τι ἔπος εἰπὼν ἀποθύμιον ἠὲ καὶ ἔρξας ,
sections.translation: offending either in word or in deed , remember to repay him double ; but if he asks you to be his friend again and be ready to give you satisfaction , welcome him . He is a worthless man who makes now one and now another his friend ; but as for you , do not let your face put your heart to shame . [*]

sections.label: Section 715
sections.original: μηδὲ πολύξεινον μηδʼ ἄξεινον καλέεσθαι ,
sections.translation: Do not get a name either as lavish or as churlish , as a friend of rogues , or as a slanderer of good men . Never dare to taunt a man with deadly poverty which eats out the heart ; it is sent by the deathless gods .

sections.label: Section 720
sections.original: φειδωλῆς , πλείστη δὲ χάρις κατὰ μέτρον ἰούσης .
sections.translation: The best treasure a man can have is a sparing tongue , and the greatest pleasure , one that moves orderly ; for if you speak evil , you yourself will soon be worse spoken of . Do not be boorish at a common feast where there are many guests ; the pleasure is greatest and the expense is least . [*] Never pour a libation of sparkling wine to Zeus after dawn

sections.label: Section 725
sections.original: χερσὶν ἀνίπτοισιν μηδʼ ἄλλοις ἀθανάτοισιν ·
sections.translation: with unwashed hands , nor to others of the deathless gods ; otherwise they do not hear your prayers but spit them back . Do not stand upright facing the sun when you make water , but remember to do this when he has set and towards his rising . And do not make water as you go , whether on the road or off the road ,

sections.label: Section 730
sections.original: μηδʼ ἀπογυμνωθείς · μακάρων τοι νύκτες ἔασιν ·
sections.translation: and do not uncover yourself : the nights belong to the blessed gods . A scrupulous man who has a wise heart sits down or goes to the wall of an enclosed court . Do not expose yourself befouled by the fireside in your house , but avoid this .

sections.label: Section 735
sections.original: μηδʼ ἀπὸ δυσφήμοιο τάφου ἀπονοστήσαντα
sections.translation: Do not beget children when you are come back from ill-omened burial , but after a festival of the gods . 737 Never cross the sweet-flowing water of ever-rolling rivers afoot until you have prayed , gazing into the soft flood , and washed your hands in the clear , lovely water .

sections.label: Section 740
sections.original: ὃς ποταμὸν διαβῇ κακότητʼ ἰδὲ χεῖρας ἄνιπτος ,
sections.translation: Whoever crosses a river with hands unwashed of wickedness , the gods are angry with him and bring trouble upon him afterwards . At a cheerful festival of the gods do not cut the withered from the quick upon that which has five branches [*] with bright steel . Never put the ladle upon the mixing-bowl

sections.label: Section 745
sections.original: πινόντων · ὀλοὴ γὰρ ἐπʼ αὐτῷ μοῖρα τέτυκται .
sections.translation: at a wine party , for malignant ill-luck is attached to that . When you are building a house , do not leave it rough hewn , or a cawing crow may settle on it and croak . Take nothing to eat or to wash with from uncharmed pots , for in them there is mischief .

sections.label: Section 750
sections.original: μηδʼ ἐπʼ ἀκινήτοισι καθιζέμεν , οὐ γὰρ ἄμεινον ,
sections.translation: Do not let a boy of twelve years sit on things which may not be moved , [*] for that is bad , and makes a man unmanly ; nor yet a child of twelve months , for that has the same effect . A man should not clean his body with water in which a woman has washed , for there is bitter mischief in that also for a time .

sections.label: Section 755
sections.original: ποινή . μηδʼ ἱεροῖσιν ἐπʼ αἰθομένοισι κυρήσας
sections.translation: When you come upon a burning sacrifice , do not make a mock of mysteries , for Heaven is angry at this also . Never make water in the mouths of rivers which flow to the sea , nor yet in springs ; but be careful to avoid this . And do not ease yourself in them : it is not well to do this .

sections.label: Section 760
sections.original: ὧδʼ ἔρδειν · δεινὴν δὲ βροτῶν ὑπαλεύεο φήμην .
sections.translation: So do : and avoid the talk of men . For Talk is mischievous , light , and easily raised , but hard to bear and difficult to be rid of . Talk never wholly dies away when many people voice her : even Talk is in some ways divine . 765 Mark the days which come from Zeus , duly telling your slaves of them , and that the thirtieth day of the month is best for one to look over the work and to deal out supplies .

sections.label: Section 769
sections.original: αἵδε γὰρ ἡμέραι εἰσὶ Διὸς πάρα μητιόεντος ,
sections.translation: For these are days which come from Zeus the all-wise ,

sections.label: Section 768
sections.original: εὖτʼ ἂν ἀληθείην λαοὶ κρίνοντες ἄγωσιν .
sections.translation: when men discern aright .

sections.label: Section 770
sections.original: Πρῶτον ἔνη τετράς τε καὶ ἑβδόμη ἱερὸν ἦμαρ ·
sections.translation: on which Leto bore Apollo with the blade of gold — each is a holy day . The eighth and the ninth , two days at least of the waxing month , [*] are especially good for the works of man . Also the eleventh and twelfth are both excellent ,

sections.label: Section 775
sections.original: ἠμὲν ὄις πείκειν ἠδʼ εὔφρονα καρπὸν ἀμᾶσθαι ·
sections.translation: alike for shearing sheep and for reaping the kindly fruits ; but the twelfth is much better than the eleventh , for on it the airy-swinging spider spins its web in full day , and then the Wise One , [*] gathers her pile . On that day a woman should set up her loom and get forward with her work .

sections.label: Section 780
sections.original: Μηνὸς δʼ ἱσταμένου τρισκαιδεκάτην ἀλέασθαι
sections.translation: Avoid the thirteenth of the waxing month for beginning to sow : yet it is the best day for setting plants . The sixth of the mid-month is very unfavorable for plants , but is good for the birth of males , though unfavorable for a girl either to be born at all or to be married .

sections.label: Section 785
sections.original: οὐδὲ μὲν ἡ πρώτη ἕκτη κούρῃ γε γενέσθαι
sections.translation: Nor is the first sixth a fit day for a girl to be born , but a kindly for gelding kids and sheep and for fencing in a sheep cote . It is favorable for the birth of a boy , but such will be fond of sharp speech , lies , cunning words , and stealthy conversation .

sections.label: Section 790
sections.original: μηνὸς δʼ ὀγδοάτῃ κάπρον καὶ βοῦν ἐρίμυκον
sections.translation: On the eighth of the month geld the boar and loud-bellowing bull , but hard-working mules on the twelfth . On the great twentieth , in full day , a wise man should be born . Such a one is very sound-witted . The tenth is favorable for a male to be born ; but , for a girl , the fourth day

sections.label: Section 795
sections.original: μέσση · τῇ δέ τε μῆλα καὶ εἰλίποδας ἕλικας βοῦς
sections.translation: of the mid-month . On that day tame sheep and shambling , horned oxen , and the sharp-fanged dog and hardy mules to the touch of the hand . But take care to avoid troubles which eat out the heart on the fourth of the beginning and ending of the month ; it is a day very fraught with fate . 800

sections.label: Section 800
sections.original: Ἐν δὲ τετάρτῃ μηνὸς ἄγεσθαι οἶκον ἄκοιτιν
sections.translation: On the fourth of the month bring home your bride , but choose the omens which are best for this business . Avoid fifth days : they are unkindly and terrible . On a fifth , they say , the Erinyes assisted at the birth of Horcus ( Oath ) whom Eris ( Strife ) bore to trouble the forsworn .

sections.label: Section 805
sections.original: Μέσσῃ δʼ ἑβδομάτῃ Δημήτερος ἱερὸν ἀκτὴν
sections.translation: Look about you very carefully and throw out Demeter ' s holy grain upon the well-rolled [*] threshing floor on the seventh of the mid-month . Let the woodman cut beams for house building and plenty of ships ' timbers , such as are suitable for ships . On the fourth day begin to build narrow ships .

sections.label: Section 810
sections.original: εἰνὰς δʼ ἡ μέσση ἐπὶ δείελα λώιον ἦμαρ ,
sections.translation: The ninth of the mid-month improves towards evening ; but the first ninth of all is quite harmless for men . It is a good day on which to beget or to be born both for a male and a female : it is never a wholly evil day . Again , few know that the twenty-seventh of the month is best

sections.label: Section 815
sections.original: ἄρξασθαί τε πίθου καὶ ἐπὶ ζυγὸν αὐχένι θεῖναι
sections.translation: for opening a wine jar , and putting yokes on the necks of oxen and mules and swift-footed horses , and for hauling a swift ship of many thwarts down to the sparkling sea ; few call it by its right name . On the fourth day open a jar . The fourth of the mid-month is a day holy above all . And again , few men know that the fourth day after the twentieth is best while it is morning :

sections.label: Section 820
sections.original: μέσση · παῦροι δʼ αὖτε μετʼ εἰκάδα μηνὸς ἀρίστην
sections.translation: towards evening it is less good . These days are a great blessing to men on earth ; but the rest are changeable , luckless , and bring nothing . Everyone praises a different day but few know their nature . Sometimes a day is a stepmother , sometimes a mother .

sections.label: Section 825
sections.original: ἄλλοτε μητρυιὴ πέλει ἡμέρη , ἄλλοτε μήτηρ .
sections.translation: That man is happy and lucky in them who knows all these things and does his work without offending the deathless gods , who discerns the omens of birds and avoids transgression .
